comment_text.sn42.11:1.1: Uruvelakappa of the Mallas is mentioned a few times in the Suttas (SN 48.52:1.2, AN 9.41:1.1). Elsewhere I can only find an Urubilvākalpa in the Lalitavistara (Vaidya 297), which however is apparently a town past Gāyā in Magadha.
comment_text.sn42.11:1.3: While this might seem like a generic question, the phrase “origin and disappearance of suffering” appears elsewhere only in the context of dependent origination (SN 12.43:1.2, SN 35.106:1.1). It is unusual to see an obscure lay person directly asking on this topic. The Buddha’s response shows that he did not think Bhadraka would understand the standard explanation, in terms of the arising of sense activity leading to continuation of rebirth. But rather than dismissing the problem as too hard, he takes an approach that Bhadraka can relate to.
comment_text.sn42.11:1.5: Dependent origination is realized at the time of stream-entry, when a person finally overcomes all doubt, including doubts such as “Did I exist in the past?” or “Will I exist on the future?”
comment_text.sn42.11:2.2: Notice that, while the Buddha is focusing on the present, he does not mean narrowly, “the present moment arisen in awareness”. It is a broader sense of present requiring reflection and inference regarding hypotheticals.
comment_text.sn42.11:2.7: The Buddha has led him to realize for himself the crucial link in dependent origination: suffering stems from craving.
comment_text.sn42.11:2.9: Compare the “inferential knowledge” of SN 12.33. The Buddha wants him to understand that this dynamic does not just apply to his present situation, but to any possible life in the past or future.
comment_text.sn42.11:2.16: The Buddha has not in fact said this exact line, although he subsequently endorses Bhadraka’s words. Perhaps the phrase has been lost from the text above.
comment_text.sn42.11:2.17: While Bhadraka endorses the Buddha’s teaching, his response is still limited to his experience in this life, despite the Buddha urging him to apply it to past and future. This confirms the Buddha’s intuition that Bhadraka was not ready to understand the deeper principles of the Dhamma.

root_text.sn42.11:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā mallesu viharati uruvelakappaṁ nāma mallānaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn42.11:1.2: Atha kho bhadrako gāmaṇi yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho bhadrako gāmaṇi bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn42.11:1.3: “sādhu me, bhante, bhagavā dukkhassa samudayañca atthaṅgamañca desetū”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:1.4: “Ahañce te, gāmaṇi, atītamaddhānaṁ ārabbha dukkhassa samudayañca atthaṅgamañca deseyyaṁ:
root_text.sn42.11:1.5: ‘evaṁ ahosi atītamaddhānan’ti, tatra te siyā kaṅkhā, siyā vimati.
root_text.sn42.11:1.6: Ahañce te, gāmaṇi, anāgatamaddhānaṁ ārabbha dukkhassa samudayañca atthaṅgamañca deseyyaṁ:
root_text.sn42.11:1.7: ‘evaṁ bhavissati anāgatamaddhānan’ti, tatrāpi te siyā kaṅkhā, siyā vimati.
root_text.sn42.11:1.8: Api cāhaṁ, gāmaṇi, idheva nisinno ettheva te nisinnassa dukkhassa samudayañca atthaṅgamañca desessāmi.
root_text.sn42.11:1.9: Taṁ suṇāhi, sādhukaṁ manasi karohi; bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:1.10: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho bhadrako gāmaṇi bhagavato paccassosi.
root_text.sn42.11:2.2: atthi te uruvelakappe manussā yesaṁ te vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:2.3: “Atthi me, bhante, uruvelakappe manussā yesaṁ me vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:2.4: “Atthi pana te, gāmaṇi, uruvelakappe manussā yesaṁ te vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā nuppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:2.5: “Atthi me, bhante, uruvelakappe manussā yesaṁ me vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā nuppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:2.6: “Ko nu kho, gāmaṇi, hetu, ko paccayo yena te ekaccānaṁ uruvelakappiyānaṁ manussānaṁ vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:2.7: “Yesaṁ me, bhante, uruvelakappiyānaṁ manussānaṁ vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā, atthi me tesu chandarāgo.
root_text.sn42.11:2.8: Yesaṁ pana, bhante, uruvelakappiyānaṁ manussānaṁ vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā nuppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā, natthi me tesu chandarāgo”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:2.9: “Iminā tvaṁ, gāmaṇi, dhammena diṭṭhena viditena akālikena pattena pariyogāḷhena atītānāgate nayaṁ nehi:
root_text.sn42.11:2.10: ‘yaṁ kho kiñci atītamaddhānaṁ dukkhaṁ uppajjamānaṁ uppajji sabbaṁ taṁ chandamūlakaṁ chandanidānaṁ.
root_text.sn42.11:2.12: Yampi hi kiñci anāgatamaddhānaṁ dukkhaṁ uppajjamānaṁ uppajjissati, sabbaṁ taṁ chandamūlakaṁ chandanidānaṁ.
root_text.sn42.11:2.16: ‘yaṁ kiñci dukkhaṁ uppajjamānaṁ uppajjati, sabbaṁ taṁ chandamūlakaṁ chandanidānaṁ.
root_text.sn42.11:2.18: Atthi me, bhante, ciravāsī nāma kumāro bahi āvasathe paṭivasati.
root_text.sn42.11:2.19: So khvāhaṁ, bhante, kālasseva vuṭṭhāya purisaṁ uyyojemi:
root_text.sn42.11:2.21: Yāvakīvañca, bhante, so puriso nāgacchati, tassa me hoteva aññathattaṁ:
root_text.sn42.11:3.2: ciravāsissa kumārassa vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:3.3: “Ciravāsissa me, bhante, kumārassa vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā jīvitassapi siyā aññathattaṁ, kiṁ pana me nuppajjissanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:3.5: ‘yaṁ kiñci dukkhaṁ uppajjamānaṁ uppajjati, sabbaṁ taṁ chandamūlakaṁ chandanidānaṁ.
root_text.sn42.11:4.2: yadā te ciravāsimātā adiṭṭhā ahosi, assutā ahosi, te ciravāsimātuyā chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:4.4: “Dassanaṁ vā te, gāmaṇi, āgamma savanaṁ vā evaṁ te ahosi:
root_text.sn42.11:4.5: ‘ciravāsimātuyā chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā’”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:5.2: ciravāsimātuyā te vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti?
root_text.sn42.11:5.3: “Ciravāsimātuyā me, bhante, vadhena vā bandhena vā jāniyā vā garahāya vā jīvitassapi siyā aññathattaṁ, kiṁ pana me nuppajjissanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti.
root_text.sn42.11:5.5: ‘yaṁ kiñci dukkhaṁ uppajjamānaṁ uppajjati, sabbaṁ taṁ chandamūlakaṁ chandanidānaṁ.

translation_text.sn42.11:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Mallas, near the Mallian town named Uruvelakappa.
translation_text.sn42.11:1.2: Then Bhadraka the village chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn42.11:1.3: “Please, sir, let the Blessed One teach me the origin and disappearance of suffering.”
translation_text.sn42.11:1.4: “Chief, if I were to teach you about the origin and disappearance of suffering in connection with the past, saying
translation_text.sn42.11:1.5: ‘this is how it was in the past,’ you might have doubts or uncertainties about that.
translation_text.sn42.11:1.6: If I were to teach you about the origin and disappearance of suffering in connection with the future, saying
translation_text.sn42.11:1.7: ‘this is how it will be in the future,’ you might have doubts or uncertainties about that.
translation_text.sn42.11:1.8: Rather, chief, I will teach you about the origin and disappearance of suffering as I am sitting right here and you are sitting right there.
translation_text.sn42.11:1.9: Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn42.11:2.2: Are there any people here in Uruvelakappa who, if they were executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, it would cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?”
translation_text.sn42.11:2.4: “But are there any people here in Uruvelakappa who, if they were executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, it would not cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?”
translation_text.sn42.11:2.6: “What’s the cause, chief, what’s the reason why, if this was to happen to some people it could cause you sorrow, while if it happens to others it does not?”
translation_text.sn42.11:2.7: “The people regarding whom this would give rise to sorrow are those I have desire and passion.
translation_text.sn42.11:2.8: The people regarding whom this would not give rise to sorrow are those I don’t have desire and passion.”
translation_text.sn42.11:2.9: “With this present phenomenon that you have seen, understood, immediately attained, and fathomed, you can apply the same method to the past and future:
translation_text.sn42.11:2.10: ‘All the suffering that arose in the past was rooted and sourced in desire.
translation_text.sn42.11:2.11: For desire is the root of suffering.
translation_text.sn42.11:2.12: All the suffering that will arise in the future will be rooted and sourced in desire.
translation_text.sn42.11:2.13: For desire is the root of suffering.’”
translation_text.sn42.11:2.15: How well said this was by the Buddha!
translation_text.sn42.11:2.16: ‘All the suffering that arises is rooted and sourced in desire.
translation_text.sn42.11:2.17: For desire is the root of suffering.’
translation_text.sn42.11:2.18: I have a boy called Ciravāsi, who resides in a house away from here.
translation_text.sn42.11:2.19: I rise early and dispatch someone, saying:
translation_text.sn42.11:2.20: ‘Go, I must say, and check on my boy Ciravāsi.’
translation_text.sn42.11:2.21: Until they get back I worry:
translation_text.sn42.11:3.2: If Ciravāsi was executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, would it cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?”
translation_text.sn42.11:3.4: “This too is a way to understand:
translation_text.sn42.11:3.5: ‘All the suffering that arises is rooted and sourced in desire.
translation_text.sn42.11:3.6: For desire is the root of suffering.’
translation_text.sn42.11:4.2: Before you’d seen or heard of Ciravāsi’s mother, did you have any desire or passion or fondness for her?”
translation_text.sn42.11:4.4: “Then was it because you saw or heard of her that you had
translation_text.sn42.11:4.5: desire or passion or fondness for her?”
translation_text.sn42.11:5.2: If Ciravāsi’s mother was executed, imprisoned, fined, or condemned, would it cause you sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress?”
translation_text.sn42.11:5.4: “This too is a way to understand:
translation_text.sn42.11:5.5: ‘All the suffering that arises is rooted and sourced in desire.
translation_text.sn42.11:5.6: For desire is the root of suffering.’”

variant_text.sn42.11:1.4: Ahañce → ahaṁ ca (bj, cck, sya1ed, km, pts1ed, mr)
variant_text.sn42.11:1.6: Ahañce → ahaṁ ca (bj, cck, sya1ed, km, pts1ed, mr)
